name: Owari
meaning: the tail end, the one at the edge
notes: Like Springfield in the US - multiple Owaris appear in nearly every domain. A real classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically, but the literal 'tail end' also makes it feel natural for the settlement at the far edge of a district, which is why it spread so widely.

name: Honmura
notes: Nearly every village district has a Honmura as its central village.

name: Shinmura
notes: Every domain has multiple, founded in different generations.

name: Furumura
notes: Often paired in a district with a Shinmura founded later.

name: Yamashita
meaning: below the mountain, at the mountain's foot

name: Mikawa
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) that, unusually, means exactly what it says: the country where three rivers run together. Appears in domains where rivers converge.

name: Komura
notes: Often the smaller of a paired Oumura/Komura set in a district.

name: Asahimura
notes: Paired district name is Asahi.

name: Yuhimura
notes: Paired district name is Yuhi.

name: Mizuho-no-sato
notes: Poetic register; Mizuho is a classical word for the most productive rice country.

name: Hatsuse-no-sato
notes: Hatsuse is an ancient name famous from classical poetry (later worn down to Hase); a village carrying it reads as very old, named in an era whose speech has itself passed away. The 'first rapids' reading is genuine geography: the village at the first rapids of its river.

name: Tatsuta-no-sato
notes: Tatsuta is a famous classical place from poems about autumn maples; a village borrows the celebrated name the way US towns are named after famous older places, and the borrowed name flatters the village's own maples.

name: Hakushou
notes: Perhaps named for the white-plastered storehouse walls of the manor - often the only whitewashed buildings visible above a village's thatch.

name: Kawauchi-shuku
notes: Kawauchi is the pocket of farmland enclosed inside a river bend - spared by most floods and easy to watch over, a natural site for a waystation.

name: Toushou-ji
notes: Temple names are lofty dharma-phrases ('eastern illumination'), not geography; the village simply takes its temple's name, so the meaning describes the temple's aspiration rather than the terrain.

name: Yuhi
notes: Also serves as the village district name when paired with Yuhimura.

name: Asahi
notes: Also serves as the village district name when paired with Asahimura.

name: Niwaya
notes: Household-named hamlet, built around a single defining family.

name: Hashimoto
meaning: bridge base, foot of the bridge

name: Hayashi
notes: Single-element name; the hamlet's identity is bounded by the grove itself.

name: Hamane
notes: The -ne ('root') in place names means the base or landward edge of a feature: the hamlet at the foot of the beach, where the dunes give way to fields.

name: Shimizu
notes: Very common; nearly every domain has at least one Shimizu hamlet built around a clean spring.

name: Shimoda
notes: Often paired with Ueda in the same district.

name: Iwami
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province): probably just 'the rocky country' - the -mi is an old suffix rather than literally 'viewing'. A name for rugged stony coastline, so it reads as a province first and a smaller place second.

name: Suou
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province), older than writing; the kanji were picked for sound and dignity, so the literal 'surrounding defense' is an artifact of the spelling, not a description of the place.

name: Asakawa
notes: Works at any scale; common in domains with significant river systems.

name: Kayano
notes: Kaya (silvergrass) is the roof-thatching grass: a village named for its thatch plain controlled a resource every roof in the district needs, and its cutting seasons structured the village year.

name: Akahata-mura
notes: The red field suggests iron-rich soil; the village's name may date from when its produce was distinctively colored.

name: Yabata-mura
notes: Named for fields that grow the bamboo or reed varieties used for arrow shafts.

name: Wakaba-no-sato
notes: Poetic register; suggests a village known for its spring foliage.

name: Aki-gou
notes: Aki is a classical province name ('kuni' = province, the old provinces of Japan); reusing one as a rural district name suggests an old, established area - like a US township borrowing the name of a famous older place.

name: Hokke-ji
notes: Named for the Hokke (Lotus Sutra) sect temple at its center.

name: Yokoichi
notes: Named for a secondary market that grew up alongside a larger one.

name: Inaba
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically - the literal 'standard's cause' is spelling, not meaning. In Japan it is best known from a folk tale (the White Hare of Inaba); a Rokugani province with this name might likewise be famous mostly for one story.

name: Wakasa
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province), older than writing; the kanji ('young narrows') were fitted to the sound of an ancient name whose original meaning is lost.

name: Tango
notes: 'The back half of Tan': when the old Tanba province grew too populous it was split, and the part farther from the Imperial capital became Tan-go ('Tan-behind'). In classical province names, 'front' and 'behind' always mean nearer to and farther from the capital. The same logic works in Rokugan measured from Otosan Uchi.

name: Mimasaka
notes: A classical province name, created when the province was split off from Bizen. The reading is historically irregular: the kanji ('beautiful work') were chosen to look auspicious under the edict that province names be written with exactly two fine characters, so gloss it briefly when introduced.

name: Kunisaki
meaning: country cape, the cape of the province

name: Iwakuni
notes: Reads stony and remote; fits a smaller, rugged province.

name: Magome
notes: Suggests a place where pack horses were rested or stabled along a clan road.

name: Karuizawa
notes: A well-known real mountain-uplands place name; its origin is debated (one theory: from karu, 'to haul on the back', for goods portered over the pass), and the kanji are a phonetic fit. Reads as a name from mountain-porter country.

name: Yatsugatake
notes: Ends in -take (岳, 'peak'), which sits outside the standard geographic-feature endings list; surface with a brief gloss on the peak ending when introduced.

name: Inamoto-mura
notes: Suggests a deep-history village considered the origin of rice cultivation in its district.

name: Mori-tono
notes: Supports a noble's hunting lodge near a forestry preserve.

name: Kuro-seki
notes: The 'black' suggests an old, weathered checkpoint or one built of dark stone.

name: Saigou-yashiki
notes: A retainer's or merchant's country estate, with a small village formed around its needs.

name: Hatsushimo-no-sato
notes: Poetic register; suggests an upland village where the first frosts arrive earlier than the surrounding lowland.

name: Suiyou-ji
notes: Suggests a riverside temple built where afternoon sun reflects off the water.

name: Aki-no-mori
notes: Poetic; a place known for the colors of its autumn foliage.

name: Tsukikage
notes: Evocative single-element name; fits a hamlet associated with moon-viewing or a moon-lit landmark.

name: Yamato
notes: The heartland province of the first Japanese emperors. Its original spelling used a character the court came to consider undignified, so it was re-spelled 'great harmony' by decree; the reading survives from the older name, which is why the kanji cannot produce it by any rule. A fine model for a Rokugani province whose written name was polished by Imperial order.

name: Yoshino
notes: Classical place name and genuine description ('good field'); in Japan famous for its cherry blossoms. Appropriate for a region with notable seasonal foliage.

name: Akagahara
notes: Notable for unusual reddish soil; locally distinctive enough that the name is not reused elsewhere in the same domain.

name: Ne-no-kuni
notes: Mythologically resonant: in classical cosmology Ne-no-kuni is associated with the underworld. A province carrying this name has an old, unsettling reputation.

name: Kage-no-sato
notes: A village in perpetual shadow because of the steep terrain that surrounds it. Distinctive and not reused.

name: Ichi-no-tani
notes: Carries martial historical weight; appropriate for a valley known for a famous battle or skirmish.

name: Hoshigaoka
notes: Suggests a hill used for star observation or astrological soothsaying; tags the place as the home of an astrologer or shrine.

name: Yamashiro
notes: Originally written 'behind the mountains' (as seen from the old capital); when the Imperial seat later moved into this province, the name was re-spelled with grander characters ('mountain castle') to suit its new dignity. Reads as the heartland of an old imperial seat, and a good model for renaming-by-decree in Rokugan.

name: Iyo
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province), older than writing; the kanji are almost purely phonetic, so the literal reading means nothing - the original sense of the ancient name is lost.

name: Echigo
meaning: beyond-behind, the back of the pass
notes: 'The far end of Koshi': the ancient Koshi region was split into Echizen, Etchu, and Echigo - 'Koshi-front', 'Koshi-middle', and 'Koshi-behind', ranked by distance from the Imperial capital. Echigo is the farthest, beyond the mountain passes.

name: Hizen
meaning: fertile-front, the front of the fertile lands
notes: 'The front of Hi': the old Hi-no-kuni ('land of fire', named for its volcanoes and hot springs) was split into Hizen and Higo, the halves nearer to and farther from the Imperial capital. 'Front' in classical province names always means capital-ward.

name: Tosa
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically - 'earth-aid' is an artifact of the kanji, not a meaning anyone intended for the place.

name: Sanuki
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province), older than writing; the kanji were fitted to the sound of an ancient name whose origin is uncertain.

name: Awa
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically. Two different real provinces shared the spoken name 'Awa' with different kanji - ancient names collide, just as Rokugani domains repeat names.

name: Bizen
notes: 'The front of Kibi': the old kingdom of Kibi was split into Bizen, Bicchu, and Bingo - front, middle, and back, ranked by distance from the Imperial capital ('front' = capital-ward).

name: Bingo
notes: 'The back of Kibi': the farthest-from-the-capital third of the old kingdom of Kibi, which was split into Bizen (front), Bicchu (middle), and Bingo (back).

name: Bicchu
notes: 'The middle of Kibi': central third of the old kingdom of Kibi, between Bizen (front, nearer the Imperial capital) and Bingo (back, farther).

name: Harima
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province, the roughly 60 provinces of old Japan). The name is older than writing: scribes later spelled it with kanji chosen mainly for sound, under an Imperial edict requiring every province name to be written with exactly two auspicious characters. The literal 'scattering-polishing' is an artifact of that spelling, not the reason for the name - to Rokugani ears it simply sounds ancient, its true meaning worn away.

name: Tajima
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically - the literal 'however-horse' is meaningless; the kanji only record the sound of a name older than writing.

name: Izumo
notes: A classical province name with strong mythological resonance in Japan: the province of the great shrine where the myths say the gods assemble each year. Unusually, the kanji fit the poetry - the oldest verses begin 'eight clouds rising' at Izumo. A model for a province whose name is bound to a founding myth.

name: Mino
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province); the kanji ('beautiful farmland') are an auspicious spelling of an older sound, though the province genuinely was rich agricultural country - scribes chose flattering spellings that fit when they could.

name: Totomi
notes: 'The far lake': named from the capital's point of view - the distant freshwater sea, as opposed to Omi, 'the near lake'. A pair of provinces named purely by where they stand relative to the Imperial capital, the way Rokugani places might be named from Otosan Uchi.

name: Shimousa
notes: 'Lower Fusa': the farther-from-the-capital half of the old Fusa country ('hemp land'), paired with Kazusa ('upper Fusa'). 'Upper' and 'lower' rank the halves by closeness to the Imperial capital, not by elevation.

name: Kazusa
notes: 'Upper Fusa': the old Fusa country ('hemp land') was split into upper (Kazusa) and lower (Shimousa) halves, 'upper' meaning nearer the Imperial capital by the old travel route - rank-by-closeness naming, like the front/behind province pairs.

name: Hitachi
notes: A classical province name; the kanji ('constant land') are an auspicious spelling. An old gazetteer glossed the name as 'hita-michi', 'the direct road' - the province at the far, straight end of the eastern highway.

name: Sagami
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically; the literal 'mutual pattern' is an artifact of the kanji, and the original meaning of the ancient name is lost.

name: Shinano
notes: A classical province name for upland mountain country, probably from the shina tree, whose bark was woven into cloth and rope in the uplands; the kanji ('trust-farmland') just spell the sound handsomely. Name the province for the tree its people lived off.

name: Izu
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically; possibly from an old word for 'jutting out' (it is a peninsula), which would make it plain geography hiding under an opaque spelling.

name: Settsu
notes: 'The administered port': the province containing the capital's harbor, run by a special port office - setsu 'administer' plus tsu 'port'. A rare classical province named for its function; the Rokugani parallel is a province defined by serving Otosan Uchi's harbor traffic.

name: Kawachi
meaning: river-inside, the land within the rivers
notes: A classical province name that means what it says: the land enclosed within the branching rivers. Plain geography, like Mikawa.

name: Izumi
notes: Originally just 'Spring' (izumi, one character): when an Imperial edict required every province name to be written with exactly two auspicious characters, a silent character was added to pad the spelling - written but not read. The clearest surviving fingerprint of how the edict reshaped province names, and a fine bit of bureaucratic history to reuse in Rokugan.

name: Kii
notes: Originally Ki, 'the tree country', named for its timber forests; the two-auspicious-characters edict padded it with a silent second character, giving a spelling ('chronicle-this') that no longer means anything. Name a timber province Kii and the scribes' hands are visible in the written form.

name: Hoki
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically; the literal 'elder-teaching' is an artifact of the kanji, and the ancient name's original sense is lost.

name: Hyuga
notes: 'Sun-facing': the classical province on the east-facing coast where the myths bring the sun goddess's grandson down to earth. One of the province names that is genuine description - an east-coast Rokugani province could carry it for the same reason.

name: Bungo
notes: 'The back of Toyo': the farther-from-the-capital half of the old Toyo-no-kuni ('land of abundance'), paired with Buzen ('front').

name: Buzen
notes: 'The front of Toyo': the old Toyo-no-kuni ('land of abundance') was split into Buzen (front, nearer the Imperial capital) and Bungo (behind, farther).

name: Higo
notes: 'The back of Hi': the farther-from-the-capital half of the old Hi-no-kuni ('land of fire'), paired with Hizen ('front'). 'Front' and 'behind' in classical province names rank the halves by distance from the Imperial capital.

name: Awaji
notes: 'The road to Awa': the island province on the sea route toward Awa - a name that is literally an itinerary. Rokugani equivalents would be named for what they are on the way to.

name: Oki
notes: 'The offing': islands out in the open sea - oki is the ordinary word for offshore waters. The kanji spelling ('hidden cape') adds a fitting flavor of remoteness; in Japan these were islands of exile, a use Rokugan can borrow.

name: Sado
notes: A classical province name spelled phonetically; in Japan an island long used for exile, which is the part worth stealing - an island province whose name alone means banishment to everyone who hears it.

name: Mutsu
notes: Contracted from michi-no-oku, 'the deep interior of the road': the province at the far end of the great northern highway, past which the road simply stops. A name for the last administered land before the frontier.

name: Dewa
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province): an ancient name of uncertain meaning, spelled with kanji for sound. The 'feather' character later bred folk tales of feather tribute - spellings grow their own legends, which is a very Rokugani thing for a name to do.

name: Tsushima
notes: The border island province between Japan and the continent; probably 'port island' (tsu = port), with kanji ('facing horse') fitted to the sound. A name for the province that faces the foreign shore - Rokugan's equivalent would face the Burning Sands or the sea beyond the Empire.

name: Iki
notes: A classical province name spelled phonetically: the small stepping-stone island province on the crossing to the continent.

name: Tanba
notes: A classical province name, possibly from 'red-earth fields'; the kanji ('vermillion wave') spell the sound handsomely. Later split, with the farther-from-the-capital part becoming Tango ('the back of Tan').

name: Etchu
notes: 'The middle of Koshi': central third of the ancient Koshi region, which was split into Echizen (front, nearest the Imperial capital), Etchu (middle), and Echigo (behind, farthest).

name: Echizen
notes: 'The front of Koshi': the nearest-to-the-capital third of the ancient Koshi region, with Etchu (middle) and Echigo (behind) beyond it. 'Front' in classical province names always means capital-ward.

name: Iga
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically; a small basin province ringed by mountains, whose ancient name's meaning is lost.

name: Noto
notes: A classical province name ('kuni' = province) spelled phonetically - a long peninsula province; the literal 'skill-ascending' is an artifact of the kanji.

name: Kibi
notes: Archaic name for one of the old great kingdoms that predate the province system; later split into Bizen, Bicchu, and Bingo. Appropriate when invoking deep history - the name of the land from before the Empire drew its lines.

name: Tsukushi
notes: Archaic poetic name (in real-world Japan, an old name for the whole southwestern island). Names of this class survive in poetry and priestly usage long after administrators have subdivided the land - useful when an old monk or a poem calls a region by its ancient name.

name: Hi-no-kuni
notes: Archaic poetic name; suggests a province with active volcanism or notable hot springs. In Japan it was later split into Hizen and Higo ('fire-front' and 'fire-behind'), the halves nearer to and farther from the capital.

name: Toyo-no-kuni
notes: Archaic poetic name; precedes the Buzen/Bungo split conceptually ('abundance-front' and 'abundance-behind'). The old whole-region name survives in poetry and ceremony after the administrative split.

name: Asuka
notes: The reading cannot be produced from the kanji ('flying bird') at all: court poetry always prefixed the place with the epithet 'of the flying birds', and the epithet fused into the official spelling. A name whose written form preserves a fragment of a poem - deep historical resonance, and a beautiful mechanism to reuse in Rokugan.

name: Yomi-no-kuni
meaning: land of the yellow springs, land of the dead
notes: Mythologically charged: in classical cosmology Yomi is the underworld. A province carrying this name has an old, unsettling reputation, perhaps associated with a haunted region or a famous tomb complex.

name: Tokoyo
notes: Mythologically charged: Tokoyo is the eternal realm across the sea in classical myth. Fits a remote coastal province where soothsayers report visions of an unchanging country.

name: Ashihara
notes: Classical name for the mortal world in myth (Ashihara-no-nakatsukuni). Appropriate for a province deeply identified with rice paddies and reed marshes.

name: Hon-machi
notes: Nearly every county town has a Hon-machi as its central district.

name: Shin-machi
notes: Common for the more recently developed district of a county town.

name: Shimo-machi
notes: In real-world Japan, shimo-machi often denotes the merchants' and artisans' quarter, as opposed to a yamanote samurai quarter.

name: Tera-machi
notes: A district of a county town defined by its concentration of temples; common in towns that grew up around a religious center.

name: Kura-machi
notes: The merchant warehouses district of a county town; usually fronts on a road or river for cargo access.

name: Toba-machi
notes: Toba is an old real place name of debated origin - the kanji ('bird-wing') mostly record sound. As a town district it reads as a name carried in from elsewhere, the way US towns reuse famous names like Richmond.

name: Furu-machi
notes: The oldest district of a county town, often where the original buildings still stand.

name: Tono-machi
notes: Reserved for districts of a town adjoining a noble's town manor; typically gated and patrolled separately.

name: Kaji-machi
notes: The forging district of a county town; tends to be sited downwind of the main residential district.

name: Hata-machi
notes: The weavers' district of a county town.

name: Sou-machi
notes: The herbalists' and apothecaries' district of a county town; sometimes the herbalist district adjoins the temple district.

name: Akebono-machi
notes: An evocative district name; often where the eastern walls catch the first light of morning.

name: Tora-machi
notes: A directional name, not a zoological one: the zodiac animals doubled as compass bearings, so the Tiger District is the district on the tiger bearing (east-northeast) from the town's center.

name: Hoshi-machi
notes: An evocative district name; typically the district with the shrine of a star Fortune, or the high open quarter where the night sky is clearest.

name: Tsuki-machi
notes: An evocative district name; often where the moon-viewing pavilions are sited.

name: Yotsuya-machi
notes: Named after four founding households or four notable inns at the district's corners.

name: Mukou-machi
notes: The district 'across the way' from the town's main residential quarter, often across a river or stream.

name: Suribachi-machi
notes: Named for the mortar-bowl shape of the depression the district sits in; the buildings rise on the inner slope.

name: Hagakure-machi
notes: Evocative classical name; the district is screened by old trees so well that travelers passing through often fail to notice it.

name: Nakano
notes: Very common; a name of one plain element, like a US town named simply 'Fields'. Nearly every domain has multiple Nakano hamlets and small villages.

name: Honda
notes: Very common. 'Hon-da' is the district's original rice paddies - the oldest cleared fields, as opposed to 'new fields' opened later. A settlement named Honda sits beside the land its district farmed first.

name: Take-mura
notes: Often a village whose grove of bamboo supplies the surrounding district with poles, scaffolding, and arrow shafts.

name: Naga-mura
notes: Often a village that runs along a single road or stream rather than clustering.

name: Hoshi-no-sato
notes: Evocative register; often a village associated with a clear night sky or a tradition of stargazing for soothsaying.

name: Hatsuyuki-mura
notes: An upland village where the first snow of the season arrives early.

name: Tsuyu-mura
notes: Evocative; often a village in a hollow where morning dew lingers later than elsewhere.

name: Iwagaki-mura
notes: Named for the natural rock formations or dry-stone walls that bound the village's fields.

name: Sakae-ji
notes: Named for the Sakae-ji temple at its center; the temple is presumably named for an early benefactor.

name: Yumi-mura
notes: A village whose principal craft is bow-making; typically near a grove of suitable wood (yew or zelkova).

name: Mukashi-no-sato
notes: Evocative; suggests a village so old its founding has slipped into legend, or one where the local headsman insists nothing has changed in seven generations.

name: Shimotsuke-shou
notes: Shimotsuke ('lower Keno') was one of a pair of classical provinces made by splitting the old Keno country into upper and lower halves ranked by distance from the capital. Reused here as an estate name; an estate village that may once have been a sub-domain in its own right.

name: Kitsune-no-sato
notes: Distinctly Fox-flavored; appropriate within Fox clan lands or in a village with a famous local fox-spirit shrine. Distinctive enough that it should not be reused within the same domain.
